original_abstract:
As Generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) advances, our digital and physical environments are increasingly permeated by synthetic content, giving rise to what can be termed a "synthetic society." This book explores the technological design, practical applications, and profound societal implications of four transformative AI-driven fields: humanoid robots, deepfakes, augmented reality, and virtual reality. While these innovations offer significant benefits in sectors such as education, healthcare, and manufacturing, they also pose severe risks, including the erosion of public trust, the proliferation of disinformation, and the potential for new forms of exploitation and manipulation.

The author critically examines the adequacy of the existing European legal framework—encompassing data protection, intellectual property, human rights, and liability law—in addressing these challenges. By identifying critical regulatory gaps and evaluating prospective legislative responses like the AI Act, the book highlights the complex trade-offs between fostering innovation and safeguarding fundamental values. Ultimately, it serves as a comprehensive guide for legal scholars, policymakers, and practitioners navigating the uneasy questions and imperfect solutions inherent in governing an AI-generated reality.
humanized_abstract:
The world is rapidly becoming submerged in a “synthetic society” of vast quantities of digital and sometimes physical synthetic products created using ever-evolving Generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) technologies. This book outlines the technological aspects, usage, and implications of four recent revolutionary applications of these technologies that will have lasting impacts on the world. Examples include such as human-looking humanoid robots, the “acting” deepfakes, as well as virtual and augmented reality. All have many different important and useful applications in different fields of the world including also for instance education, healthcare, and even production. At the same time, they all also bring huge and new risks, societal dangers and dangers of social and societal control to the world. These can affect everything from the public as consumers of for instance news and movies to from our social security and democratic bases and to our selfdefense, and maybe also to the balances of markets and the competitiveness of countries, etc.

The book provides a nuanced and systematically founded critique of current European law, including data protection, intellectual property, fundamental rights and liability law. Based on a detailed analysis of current regulatory challenges, with a view to regulatory gaps and ongoing developments like the AI Act, the book brings to light the underlying tensions and difficult choices policymakers have to navigate when seeking to pursue both innovation and protection of fundamental values in the context of a society shaped by, and increasingly governed by, the use of AI. As such, the book is an indispensable source of reference for legal scholars, for policymakers interested in the challenges of governing new technologies and for lawyers looking for some guidance as to how to navigate and critically reflect on the uneasy questions and unsatisfactory solutions raised by these technologies, and in particular by the AI-generated world around us.
